scope: First- and third-party bad faith for individual (non-commercial) P&C insureds; 50 states + DC.
rubric_version: v0.3 (Factor 11 removed; weights F2=14, F4=12, F6=12, F10=10)

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law tort exists (Chavers/Brechbill) but requires intentional refusal AND actual knowledge of no lawful basis — narrower than 'no reasonable basis' standard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Chavers v. Nat'l Sec. (Ala. 1981); Brechbill (Ala. 2013)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third-party failure-to-settle action recognized requiring excess judgment; insurer liable on negligence or intentional refusal.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Aetna Cas. & Sur. v. Mitchell Bros. (Ala. 2001); Chavers (Ala. 1981)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Alabama has not adopted UCSPA; no statutory PRoA.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Requires intentional refusal plus actual knowledge of absence of legitimate reason — knowing/reckless-disregard tier.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential, mental distress, and economic loss recoverable.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Chavers, 405 So. 2d 1; Lavoie, 470 So. 2d 1060 (Ala. 1984)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives available but capped at greater of 3x compensatory or $500k.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory penalty/multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Fees generally not recoverable in coverage litigation.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI weak/mediation-only; no dedicated consumer ombudsman.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable doctrine (Brechbill 2013 reaffirmed Bowen/Chavers); no major recent shifts.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Tort recognized (Nicholson) on 'no reasonable basis' standard — does not require fraud or deceit.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: State Farm v. Nicholson, 777 P.2d 1152 (Alaska 1989); Hillman (Alaska 1993)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Strong duty: insurer must investigate, inform of offers, and tender limits when substantial likelihood of excess verdict; excess judgment recoverable.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Whitney v. State Farm (Alaska 2011); Jackson v. Am. Equity (Alaska 2004)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: UCSPA adopted but no express or implied PRoA.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Alaska Stat. §21.36.125; O.K. Lumber v. Providence Wash. (Alaska 1998)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'No reasonable basis' objective standard; no fraud/deceit required.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Broad consequential damages (mental anxiety, credit/reputation, lost earnings) plus emotional distress without 'severe' threshold.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Alaska Pac. Assur. v. Collins (Alaska 1990); Ace v. Aetna (9th Cir. 1998)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives on clear and convincing evidence of outrageous/malice/reckless indifference.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Great Divide Ins. v. Carpenter (Alaska 2004); Alaska Stat. §09.17.020

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 10.5% statutory interest (3% + Fed disc.); no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Civil Rule 82 prevailing-party fee formula favors prevailing insured but only partial; not full one-way.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI Consumer Services Section moderate; investigates and fines but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; insufficient detail in survey on recent direction.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Robust Noble/Rawlings/Clearwater tort; objective unreasonableness with knowing/reckless mental state.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Noble v. Nat'l Am. (Ariz. 1981); Rawlings v. Apodaca (Ariz. 1986); Clearwater (Ariz. 1990)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Equal-consideration standard with multi-factor test; established cause of action.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Clearwater, 792 P.2d 719 (Ariz. 1990)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: UCSPA adopted but explicitly disclaims private cause of action.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Acts unreasonably PLUS knowingly or with reckless disregard — Anderson-style standard.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages broadly available even absent severe distress.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Farr v. Transamerica (Ariz. App. 1984)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on clear and convincing 'evil mind' showing — high bar but no statutory cap noted.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 10% statutory interest under §44-1201; no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Discretionary under §12-341.01; no mandatory one-way shift.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Consumer Affairs Division moderate; fines and C&D but no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; no major recent narrowing in survey.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Tort exists but narrow — requires 'dishonest, malicious, or oppressive' conduct with hatred/ill will/spirit of revenge.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Parker v. S. Farm Bureau (Ark. 1996); Columbia Nat'l v. Freeman (Ark. 2002)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Standard failure-to-settle: excess judgment recoverable for fraud, bad faith, or negligence.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: McCall v. S. Farm Bureau (Ark. 1973)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Limited statutory PRoA under §23-79-208 for failure to pay within policy time after demand.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Hatred, ill will, or spirit of revenge — actual-malice tier.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages may flow from bad faith but not in pure breach-of-contract; limited emotional.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Carpenter (8th Cir. 1995); H&H Brokerage (8th Cir. 1999)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available where conduct intentionally dishonest or deceitful.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 12% penalty on amount of loss + attorney fees under §23-79-208.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees to insured under §23-79-208 prosecution and collection.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Moderate — DOI investigates and fines.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; insufficient detail in survey on recent direction.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Robust common-law tort (Gruenberg/Egan); broad damages; lenient unreasonable-or-without-proper-cause standard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Gruenberg v. Aetna (Cal. 1973); Egan v. Mut. of Omaha (Cal. 1979)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Crisci/Comunale/Johansen failure-to-settle doctrine well-established with reasonable-offer rule.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Crisci v. Security (Cal. 1967); Johansen (Cal. 1975)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Royal Globe PRoA repealed by Moradi-Shalal (1988); §790.03 regulatory-only. UCL channel narrow.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Objective 'unreasonable or without proper cause'; no malice/intent required for liability (only for punitives).
f3_liability_standard.cite: Gruenberg, 510 P.2d 1032; R&B Auto Ctr. (Cal. App. 2006)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Full consequential and emotional distress damages broadly available.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Behnke (Cal. App. 2011); Cal. Civ. §3300; Cates Const. (Cal. 1999)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on clear and convincing evidence of oppression, fraud, or malice; no statutory cap on bad-faith punitives.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 10% prejudgment interest under §3287; no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Brandt fees recoverable as damages but not statutory mandatory one-way.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Brandt v. Superior Court (Cal. 1985); Cassim v. Allstate (Cal. 2004)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: CDI Hearing Bureau and Market Conduct robust on admin side.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable to mildly broadening; Nickerson 2016 reinforced punitives availability; no major recent narrowing.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Strong common-law tort (Goodson/Savio) PLUS statutory unreasonable-delay claim under §10-3-1115.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Goodson v. Am. Standard (Colo. 2004); Travelers v. Savio (Colo. 1985)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Established third-party tort under Trimble; reasonableness-under-circumstances standard.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Farmers Group v. Trimble (Colo. 1984)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: §10-3-1115/1116 PRoA with broad 'unreasonable delay or denial' trigger; reaches first-party squarely; CCPA crossover for trebling.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Common-law: unreasonable + knowing/reckless; statutory claim only requires 'without reasonable basis'.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential plus emotional distress; lost earnings recoverable.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Goodson, 89 P.3d 409 (Colo. 2004)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available with fraud/malice/willful-wanton; CCPA crossover allows treble actual damages.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 2x covered benefit under §10-3-1116 — significant statutory teeth on lower threshold.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees in statutory bad-faith and CCPA actions; not in common-law tort alone.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Division of Insurance Consumer Services moderate; fines available.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Fisher 2018 affirmed broad statutory bad-faith reading; recent precedent broadening.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law tort exists but requires 'improper motive' or 'dishonest purpose'; CUIPA/CUTPA channel adds statutory teeth.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: PSE Consulting v. Mercede (Conn. 2004); Dorfman v. Smith (Conn. 2022)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Recognized cause of action for negligent failure to settle; ordinary-prudence standard.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Carford v. Empire Fire (Conn. Super. 2012)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No direct CUIPA PRoA but CUTPA channel reaches insurance practices with treble damages and fees.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Improper motive/dishonest purpose — knowing/reckless tier; closer to actual fraud-adjacent.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages available.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Capstone Bldg. v. Am. Motorists (Conn. 2013); Buckman (Conn. 1987)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available only via CUTPA channel; treble under §42-110g.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Treble damages possible via CUTPA; 10% statutory interest.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Available under CUTPA and as compensatory damages for bad faith conduct.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No statutory pre-suit notice; CUTPA demand customary but not mandatory.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: CID Consumer Affairs moderate; fines and C&D.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Dorfman 2022 reaffirmed standard; stable.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: First-party bad-faith recognized as breach of implied covenant; 'clearly lacks reasonable justification' standard, treated as contract claim.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Enrique v. State Farm (Del. 2016); Tackett (Del. 1995)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Established failure-to-settle action for excess judgment; reasonable-and-prudent-person standard.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Stilwell v. Parsons (Del. 1958); Corrado Bros. (Del. 1989)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: UCSPA adopted but no PRoA.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Clearly lacks reasonable justification' — objective no-reasonable-basis tier.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available but emotional distress generally requires physical injury.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Tackett (Del. 1995); Pierce (Del. 1996)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on showing of malice or reckless indifference.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Legal rate (5% over Fed disc.); no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Available in bad faith and under §4102 for property insurance judgments.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI Consumer Services moderate; fines; binding arb available.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Enrique 2016 stable; no major recent shift.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: DC does not recognize tort of bad-faith claims handling in first-party context — contract remedies only.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Choharis v. State Farm (D.C. 2008)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: No instructive authority; Choharis casts doubt; would likely look to MD which recognizes one.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Choharis, 961 A.2d 1080 (D.C. 2008)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: DC has not adopted UCSPA; §31-2231.17 prohibitions explicitly do not create PRoA.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: No tort exists; contract standard only.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Insured limited to contract damages only — no consequential beyond breach-of-contract foreseeability.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Nkpado v. Standard Ins. (D.D.C. 2010)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives only where breach 'merges with willful tort' — narrow.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 6% statutory interest under §28-3302; no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Discretionary; no one-way statutory shift.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DISB Consumer Services weak; primarily referral.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Choharis 2008 firmly closes the door on first-party tort.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No common-law first-party tort; Civil Remedy Statute §624.155 provides the path but post-SB 2A (2022) requires adverse adjudication on contract first.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Time Ins. v. Burger (Fla. 1998); Fla. Stat. §624.1551

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Long-standing common-law third-party bad faith; Subsection 4 (90-day tender safe harbor) recently added.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Time Ins. v. Burger (Fla. 1998); Fla. Stat. §624.155(4)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Broad PRoA under §624.155 reaches first- and third-party but with 60-day CRN cure and post-2022 adverse-adjudication prerequisite.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Subsection 5 clarifies bad faith requires 'more than mere negligence'; insured's own good-faith conduct now considered.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages and emotional distress (where bad faith caused failure to receive necessary care) recoverable; can exceed policy limits.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: McLeod v. Cont'l Ins. (Fla. 1992); Fla. Stat. §624.155(11)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available only on 'general business practice' showing — willful/wanton/malicious or reckless disregard.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 12-18% statutory interest on overdue claims; no statutory multiplier itself but broad damages and fees.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under §624.155(7) and §627.428 for prevailing insureds.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: Mandatory 60-day CRN to DFS plus insurer cure window; post-2022 90-day liability tender safe harbor; residential property notice under §627.70152.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DFS Civil Remedy and mediation programs robust; mandatory residential property mediation; CRN database public.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: SB 2A (Dec 2022) substantially narrowed first-party bad faith — added adverse-adjudication prerequisite, 90-day tender safe harbor, comparative bad-faith reduction.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No common-law first-party tort; statutory claim under §33-4-6 only with 60-day demand prerequisite.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: O.C.G.A. §33-4-6; Lavoi (Ga. App. 2008)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Strong common-law third-party action under Holt 'equal consideration' rule; First Acceptance v. Hughes recently reaffirmed.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: S. Gen. Ins. v. Holt (Ga. 1992); First Acceptance v. Hughes (Ga. 2019)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: §33-4-6 PRoA for first-party only with 60-day demand; §33-4-7 third-party post-judgment.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Frivolous and unfounded refusal' — narrow; insurer's reasonable question of law/fact defeats claim.
f3_liability_standard.cite: King v. Atlanta Cas. (Ga. App. 2006); Flynt (Ga. App. 2011)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages NOT recoverable in first-party bad faith — §33-4-6 is exclusive remedy.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Wallace v. State Farm (Ga. App. 2000); Holt (Ga. 1992)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available in bad-faith actions if entitled to compensatory.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Howell v. S. Heritage Ins. (Ga. App. 1994)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 50% of loss or $5,000 (greater) plus attorney fees under §33-4-6.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under §33-4-6.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: Mandatory 60-day demand prerequisite with cure window.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: OCI Consumer Services moderate; fines.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: First Acceptance v. Hughes (2019) reaffirmed third-party doctrine; first-party narrow but stable.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Best Place adopted Gruenberg test — withholding benefits without proper cause; UCSPA evidence supports common-law claim.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Best Place v. Penn America (Haw. 1996)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law bad faith available; insufficient detail on third-party-specific doctrine in survey.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Best Place (Haw. 1996)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: UCSPA adopted but no PRoA; statutory violations only support common-law claim.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Unreasonable or without proper cause — objective standard adopted from California.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages available.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Best Place (Haw. 1996); Young v. Allstate (Haw. 2008)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on clear and convincing evidence of wanton/oppressive/willful misconduct.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 10% statutory interest under §478-2; no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Available under §431:10-242 for prevailing policyholder.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Insurance Division Investigations moderate; fines.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; insufficient detail in survey on recent direction.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Tort recognized under 'fairly debatable' standard; requires intentional and unreasonable denial plus harm not fully compensable by contract.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: White v. Unigard (Idaho 1986); Weinstein (Idaho 2010)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Equal-consideration standard with seven factors; recognized that fairly-debatable standard inadequate for third-party.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Truck Ins. Exch. v. Bishara (Idaho 1996); McKinley (Idaho 2007)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: UCSPA adopted but no PRoA — common-law tort suffices.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Intentional and unreasonable denial, claim not fairly debatable, not a good-faith mistake — Anderson-tier.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Tort damages including unforeseeable extra-contractual loss and emotional distress.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: White (Idaho 1986); Walston (Idaho 1996)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on clear and convincing oppressive/fraudulent/malicious/outrageous conduct.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 12% statutory interest; no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under §41-1839 if insurer fails to pay within 30 days of proof of loss.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: Proof of loss + 30-day wait under §41-1839 acts as soft pre-suit window for fees.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI Consumer Affairs moderate; fines.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable since Weinstein 2010.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No common-law first-party tort recognized; §155 preempts. Statutory remedy only with capped penalty.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Cramer v. Insurance Exchange Agency, 675 N.E.2d 897 (Ill. 1996); 215 ILCS 5/155

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law third-party bad faith failure-to-settle well-established with punitive damages available.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Griffith v. Valor Ins., 763 N.E.2d 299 (Ill. 2001); O'Neill v. Gallant Ins. Co., 769 N.E.2d 100 (Ill. 2002)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: 215 ILCS 5/155 statutory penalty path with capped damages, first-party only and exclusive remedy precluding broader tort.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Unreasonable and vexatious' standard under §155; no recovery for bona fide coverage disputes or legitimate defenses.
f3_liability_standard.cite: 215 ILCS 5/155; Scottsdale Indem. v. Village of Crestwood, 784 F.Supp.2d 988 (N.D. Ill. 2011)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages NOT available under §155 statutory claim; capped statutory penalty only.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Cramer, 675 N.E.2d 897; Nelles v. State Farm, 742 N.E.2d 420 (Ill. 2000)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives unavailable in first-party §155 claim; available only in third-party failure-to-settle context.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: O'Neill v. Gallant Ins. Co., 769 N.E.2d 100 (Ill. 2002)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Greater of $60k, 60% of recovery, or excess over offer + attorney fees—capped, modest.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees to insured under §155 on finding of vexatious conduct.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI moderate-robust; investigates, fines, license action, but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable §155 jurisprudence; no major recent broadening or narrowing.
f10_recent_appellate_trend.cite: Chandler v. Am. Fire & Cas. Co., 879 N.E.2d 396 (2007)

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party bad faith firmly recognized; insurer must act with dishonest purpose, ill will, or knowingly without rational basis.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Erie Ins. Co. v. Hickman, 622 N.E.2d 515 (Ind. 1993); Friedline v. Shelby Ins. Co., 774 N.E.2d 37 (Ind. 2002)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Indiana Supreme Court has not recognized third-party negligent failure-to-settle cause of action; remains narrow/uncertain.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Cincinnati Ins. Co. v. Selective Ins. Co., 2021 WL 766651 (S.D. Ind. 2021)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No private right of action under Indiana UCSPA (Ind. Code §27-4-1-4.5).

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Heightened standard requiring 'dishonest purpose, moral obliquity, furtive design, or ill will'—above mere negligence.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Johnson v. State Farm, 667 N.E.2d 802 (Ind. Ct. App. 1996); Lumberman's Mut. v. Combs, 873 N.E.2d 692 (2007)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages and emotional distress (via intentional tort exception to impact rule) recoverable.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Erie, 622 N.E.2d 515; Patel v. United Fire & Cas., 80 F.Supp.2d 948 (N.D. Ind. 2000)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives available on clear and convincing evidence of malice, fraud, gross negligence, or oppressiveness.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory penalty or multiplier; 8% statutory interest only.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Discretionary fees only via §34-52-1-1(b)(3) on finding of bad-faith litigation; American Rule otherwise.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Patel, 80 F.Supp.2d 948; Liberty Mut. v. OSI Indus., 831 N.E.2d 192 (Ind. Ct. App. 2005)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI moderate; investigates and fines but no individual adjudication or binding findings.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; no major recent broadening or narrowing.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party bad faith recognized with 'no reasonable basis' / 'fairly debatable' standard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Thornton v. Am. Interstate Ins. Co., 897 N.W.2d 455 (Iowa 2017); United Fire & Cas. v. Shelly Funeral Home, 642 N.W.2d 648 (Iowa 2002)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Standard duty-to-settle claim recognized; insurer protected if good-faith honest belief in defensible position.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Johnson v. Am. Family Mut. Ins. Co., 674 N.W.2d 88 (Iowa 2004)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No statutory PRoA under UCSPA per Seeman line.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Anderson-style: no reasonable basis for denial AND knowledge/reckless disregard of that lack.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages and emotional distress (with showing of mental suffering) recoverable.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Nassen v. National States Ins. Co., 494 N.W.2d 231 (Iowa 1992)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives available on showing of malice, fraud, gross negligence, or illegal act.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Dolan v. Aid Ins. Co., 431 N.W.2d 790 (Iowa 1998); Bradley v. W. Bend Mut. Ins. Co., 796 N.W.2d 455 (Iowa Ct. App. 2003)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 10% statutory interest; no other statutory penalty or multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Attorney fees recoverable in bad-faith actions per Clark-Petersen; somewhat discretionary scope.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Clark-Petersen Co. v. Indep. Ins. Assocs., 514 N.W.2d 912 (Iowa 1994)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Iowa Insurance Division Consumer Advocate Bureau; investigates and fines but no individual binding adjudication.
f9_admin_remedy.cite: Iowa Insurance Division Consumer Advocate Bureau

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Thornton (2017) reaffirmed standard without expansion or narrowing.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No common-law first-party bad-faith tort; only statutory attorney-fee remedy under §40-256 for refusal 'without just cause'.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Spencer v. Aetna Life & Cas., 611 P.2d 149 (Kan. 1980); Stroud v. Ozark Nat. Life, 510 P.3d 728 (Kan. App. 2022)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third-party claim available as breach-of-contract action requiring excess judgment and causal link.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Aves v. Shah, 906 P.2d 642 (Kan. 1995); Gruber v. Marshall, 482 P.3d 612 (Kan. App. 2021)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Limited statutory PRoA via §40-256 (attorney-fee statute) tied to refusal without just cause; no broader bad-faith PRoA.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Without just cause or excuse'—objective; no recovery if bona fide and reasonable factual ground for contesting.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Brown v. Combined Ins. Co., 597 P.2d 1080 (Kan. 1979); Koch v. Prudential, 470 P.2d 756 (Kan. 1970)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages recoverable; emotional distress only via separate tort of outrage with extreme/severe distress.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Mo. Med. Ins. Co. v. Wong, 676 P.2d 113 (Kan. 1984); Smith v. Welch, 967 P.2d 727 (Kan. 1998)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives only via independent tort with willful/wanton conduct, malice, or fraud—high threshold.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: K.S.A. §60-3702(c); Guarantee Abstract & Title v. Interstate Fire & Cas., 652 P.2d 665 (Kan. 1982)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory multiplier or penalty beyond 10% legal interest.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Mandatory one-way fees under §40-256 if refusal 'without just cause'.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI moderate; investigates and issues fines/C&D but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; recent decisions (Stroud 2022, Gruber 2021) reaffirm existing framework without major shift.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party bad faith and parallel statutory UCSPA cause of action under Reeder; broadly available.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Wittmer v. Jones, 864 S.W.2d 885 (Ky. 1993); State Farm v. Reeder, 763 S.W.2d 116 (Ky. 1989)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third parties may maintain UCSPA bad-faith action under §304.12-230 per Reeder—relatively rare expansion.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: State Farm v. Reeder, 763 S.W.2d 116 (Ky. 1989)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA via UCSPA (§304.12-230) reaching both first- and third-party; KCPA also available to insureds.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: KRS §304.12-230; KRS §367.220; Reeder, 763 S.W.2d 116; Stevens v. Motorist Mut., 759 S.W.2d 819 (Ky. 1988)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Wittmer test: lack of reasonable basis AND knowing/reckless disregard—Anderson-style standard.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages and emotional distress/mental anguish damages recoverable under UCSPA.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Curry v. Fireman's Fund, 784 S.W.2d 176 (Ky. 1989); Wittmer, 864 S.W.2d 885

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives available for outrageous conduct, evil motive, or reckless indifference; no statutory cap noted.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 12% statutory interest; no separate statutory multiplier or fixed penalty.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Attorney fees recoverable as damages under bad-faith; fee statute KRS §304.12-235 if delay without reasonable foundation.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI moderate; investigates and fines but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Demetre (2017) reaffirmed framework. No major recent narrowing.
f10_recent_appellate_trend.cite: Indiana Ins. Co. v. Demetre, 527 S.W.3d 12 (Ky. 2017)

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Statutory cause of action under §22:1892 (post-Act 3 2024 consolidation); requires arbitrary, capricious, or without probable cause failure to pay.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: La. R.S. §22:1892 (as amended by Act 3, 2024)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third-party settlement breach is enumerated act under §22:1892; standard duty-to-settle exposure.

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA reaching both first- and third-party; Act 3 (2024) folded §22:1973 into §22:1892, narrowing some remedies (eliminated 2x consequential).

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Arbitrary, capricious, or without probable cause' standard; objective but elevated.
f3_liability_standard.cite: La. R.S. §22:1892; LeBlanc v. Allied Tr. Ins. Co., 2022 WL 4597863 (W.D. La. 2022)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available but post-Act 3 no longer doubled; emotional distress availability uncertain under new statute.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: La. R.S. §22:1892 (Act 3, 2024); Lewis v. State Farm, 946 So.2d 708 (La. App. 2006)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Statutory 'penalty' of greater of 2x damages or $5,000 functions as quasi-punitive; no separate punitives.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: La. R.S. §22:1892; Sher v. Lafayette Ins. Co., 998 So.2d 201 (La. 2008)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 50% of loss or $5,000 (greater) under §22:1892 (and §22:1893(B)(1)) plus mandatory attorney fees.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Mandatory one-way attorney fees for arbitrary/capricious failure to pay.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: Satisfactory proof of loss + 30/60-day waiting period required before suit.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: LDI moderate; investigates and has receivership powers but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Act 3 (eff. 7/1/2024) substantially restructured regime, repealed §22:1973 (which had 2x consequential damages), narrowing some insured remedies.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law breach-of-good-faith claim sounds in contract only (Marquis); statutory cause under §2436-A available but no separate tort.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Marquis v. Farm Family Mut. Ins., 628 A.2d 644 (Me. 1993); 24-A M.R.S. §2436-A

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Maine does not recognize third-party bad faith.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Linscott v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 368 A.2d 1161 (Me. 1977)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA under §2436-A first-party only; no third-party reach.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Insurer acts 'without just cause' if it refuses to settle without reasonable basis to contest liability/damages—objective standard.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available; emotional distress generally not recoverable in contract claims.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Marquis, 628 A.2d 644; McAfee v. Wright, 651 A.2d 371 (Me. 1994)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives unavailable in contract action or under §2436-A.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Stull v. First Am. Title Ins. Co., 745 A.2d 975 (Me. 2000); Marquis, 628 A.2d 644

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Interest at 1.5% per month (18%/yr) on damages plus 1.5x interest if unfair claims practices found.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way attorney fees + costs available under §2436-A(1).

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Bureau of Insurance moderate; investigates and fines but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; no major recent broadening or narrowing.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No common-law first-party tort; statutory cause under §3-1701 with mandatory MIA admin exhaustion under §27-1001.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Md. Code, Cts. & Jud. Proc. §3-1701; Md. Code, Ins. §27-1001; Sefcik v. State Farm, 2024 WL 411899 (Md. Ct. Spec. App. 2024)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law third-party failure-to-settle claim recognized with excess-judgment exposure.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Trattner v. Nat'l W. Life Ins. Co., 2024 WL 3237075 (D. Md. 2024); Hartford Fire v. Annapolis Bay Charters, 69 F.Supp.2d 756 (D. Md. 1999)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA reaches first-party only with mandatory MIA exhaustion as gating procedure; no statutory third-party reach.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Good faith' = informed judgment based on honesty and diligence; objective evaluation of conduct/diligence.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Md. Code, Ins. §27-1001; Cecilia Schwaber Tr. Two v. Hartford, 636 F.Supp.2d 481 (D. Md. 2009)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Actual damages plus expenses, costs, attorney fees, and up to 25% interest; no punitives. Excess-judgment recoverable in third-party.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Md. Code, Cts. & Jud. Proc. §3-1701(e)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive damages unavailable for first-party statutory bad-faith claims.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Md. Code, Cts. & Jud. Proc. §3-1701; Allstate Indem. v. Parsons, 2010 WL 2163869 (D. Md. 2010)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Up to 25% interest on actual damages plus expenses—significant but capped.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way attorney fees recoverable up to 1/3 of actual damages.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: Mandatory MIA complaint required pre-suit; admin exhaustion is unique nationwide barrier.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: MIA adjudicates §27-1001 complaints with binding 90-day determination on bad faith and damages; uniquely robust admin path.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable §27-1001/§3-1701 jurisprudence; recent decisions reaffirm framework.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law bad faith plus robust statutory c. 93A/176D cause of action; well-established and broadly available.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Hopkins v. Liberty Mutual, 750 N.E.2d 943 (Mass. 2001); Green v. Blue Cross & Blue Shield, 713 N.E.2d 992 (Mass. App. Ct.)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third-party claims actionable under c. 93A via c. 176D §3(9); broad reach.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 93A §9; c. 176D §3(9); Hopkins, 750 N.E.2d 943

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Robust statutory PRoA via c. 93A §9 reaching both first- and third-party with low-procedural-friction 30-day demand and powerful remedies.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Unfair-trade-practice standard objective; insurer must make reasonable settlement tender once liability reasonably clear.
f3_liability_standard.cite: c. 176D §3(9)(f); Hopkins, 750 N.E.2d 943; Rawan v. Cont'l Cas. Co., 136 N.E.3d 327 (Mass. 2019)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: All foreseeable consequential losses + emotional distress (when insurer acts deceptively/knowingly) recoverable.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Rivera v. Com. Ins. Co., 993 N.E.2d 1208 (Mass. Ct. App. 2013); Hershenow v. Enter. Rent-A-Car, 840 N.E.2d 526 (Mass. 2006)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Up to 2x or 3x actual damages for willful/knowing 93A violation (statutory multiplier serves punitive function).

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 2x-3x damages for willful or knowing violations—among nation's strongest statutory teeth.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Mandatory one-way attorney fees + costs to prevailing insured under c. 93A.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 93A §9(4); Bobick v. U.S. Fid. & Guar., 790 N.E.2d 653 (Mass. 2003)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: 30-day c. 93A demand letter required pre-suit with reasonable-tender safe harbor.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI moderate; investigates and fines but no individual binding adjudication.
f9_admin_remedy.cite: Massachusetts Division of Insurance Consumer Service Section

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable c. 93A/176D jurisprudence; Rawan (2019) reaffirmed causation requirement without major shift.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Michigan does not recognize independent first-party bad-faith tort; only breach-of-contract claims with limited consequential damages.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Roberts v. Auto-Owners Ins. Co., 374 N.W.2d 905 (Mich. 1985); Kewin v. Mass. Mut. Life, 295 N.W.2d 50 (Mich. 1980)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Bad-faith failure-to-settle is hybrid contract claim allowing recovery of excess judgment; no independent tort.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Frankenmuth Mut. Ins. v. Keeley, 461 N.W.2d 666 (Mich. 1990); Com. Union v. Liberty Mut., 393 N.W.2d 161 (Mich. 1986)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No private right of action under Uniform Trade Practices Act; Michigan Consumer Protection Act preempted.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Kassab v. Mich. Basic Prop. Ins. Ass'n, 491 N.W.2d 545 (Mich. 1992); Mich. Comp. Laws §445.904(3)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Arbitrary, reckless, indifferent, or intentional disregard' of insured's interests—elevated standard.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages limited to those contemplated at contract; emotional distress unavailable absent independent tort.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Lawrence v. Will Darrah & Assocs., 516 N.W.2d 43 (Mich. 1994); Roberts, 374 N.W.2d 905; Burnside v. State Farm, 528 N.W.2d 749 (Mich. App. 1995)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives unavailable for first- or third-party claims; narrow exemplary-damages exception rarely applies.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Casey v. Auto Owners Ins. Co., 729 N.W.2d 277 (Mich. App. 2006); Kewin, 295 N.W.2d 50

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 12% per annum penalty interest under UTPA on late-paid claims when not reasonably in dispute.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Attorney fees unavailable for either first- or third-party claims pursuing the insurer.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Burnside, 528 N.W.2d at 752-53; Johnson v. USA Underwriters, 936 N.W.2d 834 (Mich. App. 2019)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DIFS moderate; investigates and fines but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable but anti-insured baseline; no major recent appellate shift.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No common-law first-party bad-faith tort; statutory cause under §604.18 requires court permission via motion to amend.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Minn. Stat. §604.18; Peterson v. W. Nat'l Mut. Ins. Co., 946 N.W.2d 903 (Minn. 2020)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law third-party duty-to-settle with heightened fiduciary obligations; no statutory cause.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Peterson, 946 N.W.2d 903; Fette v. Columbia Cas. Co., 1993 WL 377091 (Minn. Ct. App. 1993)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA under §604.18 first-party only with court-permission gating; capped damages.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Anderson-style: lacked reasonable basis AND knew or recklessly disregarded that lack.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Minn. Stat. §604.18, subd. 2(a); Anderson v. Continental Ins., 271 N.W.2d 368 (Wis. 1978)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available; emotional distress requires independent tort.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Pillsbury Co. v. Nat'l Union Fire, 425 N.W.2d 244 (Minn. Ct. App. 1988); Lickteig v. Alderson, 556 N.W.2d 557 (Minn. 1996)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives unavailable for first-party; available only in third-party failure-to-settle.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: In re Silicone Implant Ins. Coverage Litig., 652 N.W.2d 46 (Minn. Ct. App. 2002); Wilbur v. State Farm, 880 N.W.2d 874 (Minn. Ct. App. 2016)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Up to one-half of proceeds in excess of insurer's offer or $250k (whichever less) under §604.18.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way attorney fees up to $100k under §604.18; otherwise generally not recoverable.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Minn. Stat. §604.18(3)(2); American Standard Ins. v. Le, 551 N.W.2d 923 (Minn. 1996)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: Court permission required via motion to amend complaint with evidentiary support; effective pre-suit gate.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Commerce Dept moderate; investigates and fines but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Peterson (2020) reaffirmed framework without major shift.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party bad faith recognized but as intentional tort requiring willful/malicious conduct or gross/reckless disregard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Liberty Mut. v. McKneely, 862 So.2d 530 (Miss. 2003); Universal Life v. Veasley, 610 So.2d 290 (Miss. 1992)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Mississippi does not recognize third-party bad faith.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Hartford Underwriters Ins. Co. v. Williams, 936 So.2d 888 (Miss. 2006)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No private right of action under §83-5-45 (UCSPA).
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Miss. Code §83-5-45; Burley v. Homeowner's Warranty Corp., 773 F.Supp. 844 (S.D. Miss. 1990)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Intentional-tort standard requiring willful/malicious wrong or gross/reckless disregard; mere negligence insufficient.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages recoverable when insurer's conduct culpable; barred when insurer has arguable basis.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Veasley, 610 So.2d 290; Lisanby, 47 So.3d 1172 (Miss. 2010)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives available with showing of malice, gross negligence, or wanton disregard plus no legitimate basis.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Vaughn v. Monticello Ins. Co., 838 So.2d 983 (Miss. Ct. App. 2001); Hoover, 125 So.3d 636 (Miss. 2013)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory penalty or multiplier; 8% statutory interest only.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Attorney fees recoverable when foreseeable from insurer's conduct (extracontractual damages); not statutory one-way.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Windmon v. Marshall, 926 So.2d 867 (Miss. 2006); Veasley, 610 So.2d 290

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI weak/mediation-only; investigates and fines but no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; recent decisions reaffirm restrictive bad-faith framework.
f10_recent_appellate_trend.cite: Blanchard v. GEICO Gen. Ins. Co., 375 So.3d 723 (Miss. Ct. App. 2023); Hoover, 125 So.3d 636

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No common-law first-party bad-faith tort; statutory vexatious-refusal cause under §375.420.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Mo. Rev. Stat. §375.420; Duncan v. Andrew County Mut. Ins. Co., 665 S.W.2d 13 (Mo. Ct. App. 1983)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Robust common-law third-party bad-faith tort; insurer fiduciary duty with full excess-judgment liability and punitives available.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Shobe v. Kelly, 279 S.W.3d 203 (Mo. Ct. App. 2009); Truck Ins. Exch. v. Prairie Framing, 162 S.W.3d 64 (Mo. Ct. App. 2005)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA via §375.420 vexatious-refusal extends to both first- and third-party but with capped percent penalty.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Mo. Rev. Stat. §375.420; Drury Co. v. Missouri United Sch. Ins. Couns., 455 S.W.3d 30 (Mo. Ct. App. 2014)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: 'Without reasonable cause or excuse'—objective; insurer protected if reasonable cause to believe no liability.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Dhyne v. State Farm, 188 S.W.3d 454 (Mo. 2006); Doe Run Res. v. Lloyd's London, 400 S.W.3d 463 (Mo. Ct. App. 2013)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages, emotional distress, and full excess-judgment recoverable in third-party bad-faith.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Rinehart v. Shelter Gen. Ins. Co., 261 S.W.3d 583 (Mo. App. W.D. 2008); Truck Ins. Exch., 162 S.W.3d 64

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives available on clear and convincing showing of culpable mental state—wanton, willful, outrageous, or reckless disregard.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: John v. Allstate Ins. Co., 262 S.W.3d 655 (Mo. Ct. App. 2008); Rinehart, 261 S.W.3d 583

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Vexatious-refusal: 20% of first $1,500 + 10% over $1,500 + attorney fees—modest cap.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way attorney fees under §375.420 vexatious-refusal statute.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI moderate; investigates, fines, C&D but no individual binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; recent decisions reaffirm vexatious-refusal framework without major shift.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Both an independent statutory PRoA under §33-18-242 and a common-law tort/breach claim available to first-party insureds.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Mont. Code Ann. §33-18-242; Draggin' Y Cattle Co. v. Junkermier (Mont. 2019)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Statutory PRoA under §33-18-242 reaches third-party claimants (rare), plus common-law duty to settle within limits.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Mont. Code Ann. §33-18-242; Draggin' Y Cattle (Mont. 2019)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: §33-18-242 is one of few statutes creating an independent PRoA reaching BOTH first- and third-party claimants with no cure period.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Statute imposes liability unless insurer had a reasonable basis in law or fact for contesting; objective standard with reasonable-basis defense.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Compensatory + consequential + emotional distress (Stephens) recoverable; punitive available under MUTPA.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Mont. Code Ann. §33-18-242; Stephens v. Safeco (Mont. 1993)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive available on actual malice/fraud (clear & convincing under §27-1-221), capped at $10M.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Statutory PRoA exists but no built-in multiplier or percent penalty; only 10% interest under §31-1-106.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Attorneys' fees generally NOT recoverable in Montana per Mountain West Farm Bureau v. Hall.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Mountain West Farm Bureau v. Hall (Mont. 2001)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period; third-party must wait until underlying claim resolved (modest timing constraint).

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: CSI Policyholder Services investigates with fines and C&D, but mostly moderate role; no binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Draggin' Y (2019) reaffirmed scope but no major recent broadening or narrowing.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Recognized common-law tort for first-party bad faith under LeRette; standard requires lack of reasonable basis + knowledge/reckless disregard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: LeRette v. Am. Med. Sec. (Neb. 2005)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third-party bad faith recognized but requires more than negligence — 'some level of intentional wrongdoing' per Braesch.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Braesch v. Union Ins. (Neb. 1991); Millard Gutter (Neb. 2022)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No statutory PRoA under Nebraska's UCSPA; bad-faith remedy is purely common-law.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Anderson-style 'no reasonable basis' + knowing/reckless disregard standard; arguable basis defense.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages and emotional distress (severe and substantial) available.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Ruwe v. Farmers Mut. (Neb. 1991); Millard Gutter (Neb. 2022)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive damages unavailable in Nebraska as a constitutional matter.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory penalty/multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees available under §44-359 to prevailing insured.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI Consumer Affairs investigates with moderate enforcement; no binding individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Millard Gutter (2022) reaffirmed and modestly broadened third-party bad faith and emotional distress; trend stable to slightly broadening.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Strong common-law tort under Falline plus independent statutory PRoA via NRS 686A.310 reaching first-party.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Falline v. GNLV (Nev. 1991); NRS 686A.310; Hart v. Prudential

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law third-party bad faith well-established (Landow); standard considers welfare of insured.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Landow v. Med. Ins. Exch. (D. Nev. 1995)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: NRS 686A.310 creates PRoA per Hart v. Prudential; reaches both contexts but tied to enumerated practices.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Falline standard: no reasonable basis + knew/recklessly disregarded — Anderson/Wisconsin-style.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential + emotional distress damages broadly available; excess judgment recoverable third-party.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: U.S. Fid. & Guar. v. Peterson (Nev. 1975); Fiscus (Nev. 1986)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive available on clear & convincing oppression/fraud/malice; no cap (rare).

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Prime+2% interest under NRS 99.040; no multiplier or percent penalty.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Discretionary fees only when recovery does not exceed $20k or claim was harassment; narrow.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required for either common-law or statutory claims.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Moderate-robust DOI with fines, C&D, restitution orders; mandatory non-binding arb for auto BI <$50k.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Hart v. Prudential established statutory PRoA; no recent narrowing or major broadening.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No first-party bad-faith tort recognized (Lawton); only narrow Bennett exception when insurer had exclusive control over investigation.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Lawton v. Great SW (N.H. 1978); Bell v. Liberty Mut. (N.H. 2001); Bennett v. ITT Hartford (N.H. 2004)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Recognized third-party bad faith with negligence-style standard (reasonable person would have settled).
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Dumas v. State Farm (N.H. 1971); Gelinas (N.H. 1988); O'Meara (N.H. 2012)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: §417 has no insured PRoA absent prior Insurance Commissioner finding (Hunt/First Cir.); CPA does not apply to insurance.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: RSA 417:4; Hunt v. Golden Rule (1st Cir. 2011); Bell (N.H. 2001)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Third-party uses negligence standard (a reasonable person would have settled); first-party effectively contract.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available if foreseeable; emotional distress only with separate finding of extreme/outrageous conduct.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Bell (N.H. 2001); Jarvis (N.H. 1982)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive damages unavailable by statute.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: $2,500 per violation payable to insured if Commissioner finds §417 violation; no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Fees available in declaratory coverage actions (RSA 491:22-b) and on bad-faith promotion of litigation; not generally one-way.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No formal cure period but §417 PRoA effectively requires prior Commissioner determination — significant gating.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: NHID investigates moderately with fines and C&D; gating role for statutory claim is significant but mediation-style.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Bennett carve-out and progeny narrowly applied; no major recent direction.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party tort recognized (Pickett); 2022 Insurance Fair Conduct Act adds statutory cause for UM/UIM unreasonable delay/denial.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Pickett v. Lloyd's (N.J. 1993); N.J. Stat. §17:29BB-3

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law third-party bad faith well-established; insurer liable above policy limits if refusal not honest weighing of probabilities.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Pickett v. Lloyd's, 621 A.2d 445 (N.J. 1993)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Insurance Fair Conduct Act (2022) PRoA limited to UM/UIM first-party claims; no general PRoA per Pickett.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Anderson standard: absence of reasonable basis + knew/recklessly disregarded; 'fairly debatable' bar.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential economic damages available; emotional distress only in egregious circumstances.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Pickett, 621 A.2d at 454, 480

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive on clear & convincing actual malice or wanton/willful disregard; capped at 5x compensatory or $350k.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No general statutory multiplier; new IFCA limited to UM/UIM context.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees available under R. 4:42-9(a)(6) for liability/indemnity coverage actions; multi-factor discretion.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No statutory pre-suit notice or cure period; appraisal is contractual not statutory barrier.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Robust DOBI; statutorily-created Insurance Consumer Ombudsman (rare); mandatory PIP arbitration; fines + C&D.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Trend toward broadening: Wadeer reaffirmed; 2022 IFCA enacted creating new statutory remedy.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Both common-law tort (Sloan/Hovey-Jaramillo) and statutory PRoA under §59A-16-30; bad-faith treated as torts.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Sloan v. State Farm (N.M. 2004); N.M. Stat. §59A-16-30

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Recognized for failure to defend, settle within limits, and timely pay; entire judgment recoverable on excess.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Am. Nat'l Prop. v. Rosenschein (D.N.M. 2020); Dairyland v. Herman (10th Cir. 1998)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: §59A-16-30 broad PRoA; UPA crossover via §57-12-10 (Hovet) layers consumer-protection remedies including treble.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Frivolous/unfounded denial — i.e., arbitrary or baseless refusal lacking reasonable grounds; objective.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Sloan, 85 P.3d at 236-37; Jackson Nat'l v. Receconi (N.M. 1992)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages and excess judgment recoverable; emotional distress availability uncertain (federal courts have rejected).
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: N.M. Stat. §59A-16-30; Fava v. Liberty Mut. (D.N.M. 2018)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive available on culpable mental state; clear & convincing standard.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Supreme Contracting v. Preferred (D.N.M. 2023); Peck v. Progressive (D.N.M. 2023)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Treble damages potentially available via UPA §57-12-10 crossover; 15% statutory interest.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under §59A-16-30 if willful violation; broader award under §39-2-1 if insurer acted unreasonably.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: OSI Consumer Assistance Bureau investigates moderately with fines; no binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Hovey-Jaramillo (2023) reaffirmed bad-faith claims as torts; no major recent narrowing.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No first-party bad-faith tort (Acquista); contractual remedy with Bi-Economy consequential damages but no PRoA.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Acquista v. NY Life (N.Y. App. 2001); Bi-Economy v. Harleysville (N.Y. 2008)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Recognized duty to settle in good faith; 'gross disregard' / deliberate or reckless failure standard; no sinister motive required.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Pavia v. State Farm (N.Y. 1993); Soto (N.Y. 1994)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No statutory PRoA per Rocanova/Dalton; §2601 is regulatory only.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: N.Y. Ins. Law §2601; Rocanova v. Equitable (N.Y. 1994)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Third-party requires gross disregard — deliberate or reckless indifference; high bar above mere negligence.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available (Bi-Economy/Panasia); excess judgment recoverable third-party; emotional distress not awarded.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Bi-Economy (N.Y. 2008); Panasia (N.Y. 2008); Soto (N.Y. 1994)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive only with separate tort + 'gross/morally reprehensible' conduct directed at public; very narrow.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory multiplier; only 9% CPLR §5004 interest.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Generally not available; Mighty Midgets bars fees as damages; narrow declaratory exception.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period; mandatory PIP arbitration applies in narrow context only.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DFS Consumer Assistance moderate-robust; substantive review and complaint ratios published; mandatory PIP arb.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Bi-Economy/Panasia (2008) opened consequential damages but no recent broadening of tort or PRoA.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law bad faith recognized (Von Hagel/Lovell) plus §75-1.1 UDTPA channel via Gray; requires aggravating/outrageous conduct.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Lovell v. Nationwide (N.C. 1993); Gray v. NC Ins. Underwriting (N.C. 2000)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Recognized but channeled through same common-law/§75-1.1 framework; no separate Stowers-line elaboration.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Topsail Reef HOA v. Zurich (4th Cir. 2001)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No direct PRoA under §58-63-15; channels through Chapter 75 UDTPA per Gray with treble + fees.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Aggravating/outrageous conduct required — fraud, malice, gross negligence, oppression, wanton/reckless disregard.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages possible (federal courts allow); emotional distress in limited cases; treble under §75-16.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Bald Head Island (E.D.N.C. 2022); Dailey (N.C. App. 1985)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive available with aggravating/outrageous conduct (fraud/malice/willful or wanton); §1D-15.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Defeat the Beat v. Lloyd's (N.C. App. 2008); §1D-15

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Treble damages under §75-16 via UDTPA channel — strong but requires §75-1.1 violation.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under §75-16.1 to prevailing UDTPA party with willful violation finding.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI Consumer Services investigates moderately with fines and C&D; no binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Lifebrite (2023) reaffirmed Lovell framework; no major recent direction change.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law tort for bad faith recognized; insurer acts unreasonably by failing to compensate without proper cause.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Fetch v. Quam (N.D. 2001); Corwin Chrysler-Plymouth (N.D. 1979)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Bad-faith framework applies to insurer's contractual relationship; not separately elaborated for failure to settle.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Fetch (N.D. 2001); Corwin Chrysler (N.D. 1979)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Volk declined to determine whether §26.1-04 creates PRoA; effectively no recognized statutory route.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Test is whether insurer acts unreasonably — objective reasonable-basis standard, lower than knowing/reckless.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential, compensatory, and emotional distress damages all available; attorneys' fees recoverable as consequential.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Hartman v. Estate of Miller (N.D. 2003); Ingalls v. Paul Revere (N.D. 1997)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive available with finding of oppression/fraud/malice (actual or implied) beyond bad-faith finding.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory penalty/multiplier; only 6% interest.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Attorneys' fees recoverable as consequential damages on bad-faith finding (Hartman/Corwin) — broad common-law route.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI Consumer Assistance investigates moderately with fines; no binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; longstanding Corwin/Fetch framework continues without significant recent change.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Independent common-law bad-faith tort recognized for both first- and third-party (Zoppo); reasonable justification standard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Zoppo v. Homestead Ins. (Ohio 1994); Ballard v. Nationwide (Ohio App. 2015)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third-party bad faith recognized with same Zoppo framework.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Am. States Ins. v. Sovereign Chemical (Ohio App. 2002); Zoppo

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No statutory PRoA per Strack; §3901.21 is regulatory only.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Strack v. Westfield (Ohio App. 1986); Ohio Rev. Code §3901.21

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Reasonable justification standard (Zoppo modified prior 'actual knowledge' requirement); more permissive than malice.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Compensatory damages flowing from bad faith including emotional distress and economic harm; attorneys' fees as element when punitives warranted.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Zoppo (Ohio 1994); Eastham; Asmaro

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive available on actual malice/fraud/insult; Ohio caps apply.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory multiplier or percent penalty.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Fees as element of compensatory damages only when punitives warranted; not free-standing one-way.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: ODI Consumer Services moderate with fines and C&D; no binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Zoppo framework remains; no major recent broadening or narrowing.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Strong common-law tort under Christian/Badillo with broad damages; well-established Oklahoma doctrine.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Christian v. Am. Home Assur. (Okla. 1977); Badillo v. Mid Century (Okla. 2005)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law bad-faith framework applies; Badillo elaborated standards.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Badillo v. Mid Century (Okla. 2005)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No statutory PRoA — Walker v. Chouteau Lime expressly held no PRoA under §1250.5.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Unreasonable conduct under the circumstances — objective reasonableness, lenient than malice; what insurer knew/should have known.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Hale v. A.G. Ins. (Okla. App. 2006); Badillo (Okla. 2005)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Actual + consequential + emotional distress (no severe/outrageous threshold) + punitive — broad damages package.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Capstick v. Allstate (10th Cir. 1993); Hale (Okla. App. 2006)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive on wanton/reckless disregard; capped per 23 O.S. §9.1.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Willis v. Midland Risk (10th Cir. 1994); 23 O.S. §9.1

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory multiplier; only 6% interest.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Fees available under 36 O.S. §3629 to prevailing insured.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: OID Consumer Assistance moderate with fines and Anti-Fraud unit; no binding adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Christian/Badillo line continues; emotional distress threshold confirmed lenient.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Historically no first-party tort (Farris); Moody (2023) opened negligence-per-se theory for emotional distress under ORS 746.230, scope still developing.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Farris v. USF&G (Or. 1978); Moody v. Oregon Comm. CU (Or. 2023)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Third-party bad-faith claim recognized in tort under Georgetown Realty.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Georgetown Realty v. Home Ins. (Or. 1992); Parvin v. CNA

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No express PRoA under ORS 746.230; Moody allows negligence-per-se via UCSPA standards but contours unclear.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Negligence-style under Moody for emotional distress; contract-based reasonableness otherwise.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages limited to those reasonably contemplated; emotional distress now possible post-Moody.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Nw. Pump v. America States (Or. 1996); Moody (Or. 2023)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive damages generally unavailable in bad-faith context.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory penalty/multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under ORS 742.061 if recovery exceeds tender; broad applicability but 6-month cure window.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: ORS 742.061 effectively requires 6-month cure period after proof of loss — significant procedural friction.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DCBS Insurance Consumer Advocacy Unit moderate-robust with fines, C&D, restitution.
f9_admin_remedy.cite: Oregon DCBS Insurance Consumer Advocacy Unit

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Trending toward broadening: Moody (2023) opened negligence-per-se path; lower courts expanding.
f10_recent_appellate_trend.cite: Moody v. Oregon Comm. CU (Or. 2023); Liquid Agents (D. Or. 2024)

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Strong statutory bad-faith path under §8371 plus contract-based implied covenant; tort treated separately and non-contingent.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: 42 Pa.C.S. §8371; Ash v. Continental (Pa. 2007)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: §8371 reaches third-party scenarios; plus common-law duty under Birth Center.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: 42 Pa.C.S. §8371; Birth Center v. St. Paul (Pa. 2001)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: §8371 broad PRoA covering any bad-faith insurer conduct, no pre-suit notice, no cure period.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Terletsky test: no reasonable basis + knew/recklessly disregarded; clear and convincing standard of proof.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Compensatory damages including emotional distress plus statutory punitive damages; broad scope under Birth Center.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Birth Center v. St. Paul (Pa. 2001); 42 Pa.C.S. §8371

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Statutorily authorized punitive damages on clear and convincing bad-faith finding; no statutory cap.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Prime+3% interest on claim from date made + punitives + costs + fees — strong statutory teeth, no multiplier per se.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Mandatory one-way fees + court costs available on bad-faith finding under §8371.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure period under §8371.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: PID Bureau of Consumer Services moderate with fines and C&D; no binding individual adjudication.
f9_admin_remedy.cite: PA Insurance Department Bureau of Consumer Services

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable broad statute; Rancosky (2017) confirmed Terletsky test; Watchword (2024) clarified clear-and-convincing standard — modestly stable to slightly broadening.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Statutory PRoA under §9-1-33 plus independent common-law tort recognized; broad damages available.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: R.I. Gen. Laws §9-1-33; Skaling v. Aetna (R.I. 2002); Zarrella v. Minn. Mut. (R.I. 2003)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Asermely rule: insurer liable for excess judgment when it rejects within-limits demand absent insured's refusal — broader than typical Stowers.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Asermely v. Allstate (R.I. 1999); DeMarco v. Travelers (R.I. 2011)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA under §9-1-33 — broad in first-party context with punitive and fees baked in; third-party reached through common-law not statute.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Insurer acted unreasonably AND knew or was conscious that conduct was unreasonable — Anderson-style standard.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages broadly available, plus punitive.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Bibeault v. Hanover (R.I. 1980)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive damages available as a matter of right in bad-faith cases on showing of bad-faith refusal — relatively favorable.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No fixed multiplier or % penalty; statute incorporates punitives + fees but no automatic mathematical teeth. Stat. interest 12%.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fee shift to insured under §9-1-33 upon bad-faith finding.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No statutory pre-suit notice, demand, or cure period required.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DBR Insurance Division mediates and fines but no formal individual adjudication or pre-suit channel.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Asermely and Skaling remain controlling; no major narrowing or broadening in recent decade.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law tort recognized in Nichols with reasonable-basis liability standard; statute does not create PRoA.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Nichols v. State Farm (S.C. 1983); Masterclean v. Star Ins. (S.C. 2001)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Tyger River duty to settle when reasonable; insurer liable for entire excess judgment for unreasonable refusal.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Tyger River Pine v. Maryland Cas. (S.C. 1933); Doe v. SCMMLJUA (S.C. 2001)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: UCSPA does not create PRoA; §38-59-40 provides fees only as adjunct, not standalone PRoA. First-party only.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: No reasonable basis to support denial — objective test, negligence may be considered.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages without regard to policy limits; emotional distress recognized in federal applications but limited state authority.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Brown v. SC Ins. (S.C. App. 1984); Wright v. UNUM (D.S.C. 2001)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on willful or reckless disregard standard; no statutory cap specific to bad faith though general SC punitive caps apply.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Up to 1/3 of judgment as attorney fees under §38-59-40 — penalty-like but limited; no multiplier.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fee shift after 90-day demand on bad-faith refusal, capped at 1/3 of judgment.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: 90-day demand triggers fee statute but is not a strict pre-suit prerequisite to common-law bad faith. Modest friction.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI Office of Consumer Services mediates and fines; no individual adjudicatory mechanism.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Reeves (2021) reaffirmed Tyger River line; no major recent direction.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party bad-faith tort recognized in Champion/Stene with reasonable-basis test.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Champion v. USF&G (S.D. 1987); Stene v. State Farm (S.D. 1998)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Crabb recognizes excess-judgment cause of action for wrongful or unreasonable refusal to settle; assignable.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Crabb v. Nat'l Indem. (S.D. 1973); Trouten v. Heritage Mut. (S.D. 2001)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: UCSPA §58-33-67 does not create PRoA; §58-33-69 expressly disclaims one. §58-12-3 fees are an adjunct, not a PRoA.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: No reasonable basis + insurer's knowledge or reckless disregard — Anderson-style.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available; emotional distress conditioned on showing pecuniary loss caused the distress.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Athey v. Farmers Ins. (8th Cir. 2000)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available under SDCL §21-3-2 on oppression, fraud, or malice — clear & convincing.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No multiplier or % penalty. Statutory interest only.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under §58-12-3 if refusal vexatious or without reasonable cause.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No statutory pre-suit notice, demand, or cure period.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI is weak/mediation-focused; fines but no adjudicatory mechanism.
f9_admin_remedy.cite: SD Division of Insurance Consumer Services

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable doctrine; no recent major narrowing or broadening.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No first-party common-law tort; statutory remedy under §56-7-105 (penal, strictly construed) is exclusive statutory path though common-law contract remedies coexist.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Tenn. Code §56-7-105; Lindenberg v. Jackson Nat'l (6th Cir. 2018); Fred Simmons Trucking (Tenn. App. 2004)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law cause of action for failure to settle within limits; ordinary care/diligence standard with willingness-to-gamble framing.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Johnson v. Tennessee Farmers Mut. (Tenn. 2006)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Statutory PRoA under §56-7-105 is penal and strictly construed, first-party only, with formal demand + 60-day wait.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Tenn. Code §56-7-105; Wilson v. State Farm (E.D. Tenn. 2011)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Refusal not in good faith — strictly construed penal standard requires more than mere mistake; high bar in practice.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages available, but ED requires outrageous conduct + serious mental injury.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Holt v. Am. Progressive Life Ins. (Tenn. App. 1987); Riad v. Erie (Tenn. App. 2013)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Conflicting authority post-Riad and Lindenberg; punitives now available alongside statutory penalty if separate tort proven, clear & convincing.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Riad v. Erie (Tenn. App. 2013); Lindenberg (6th Cir. 2018)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 25% of liability penalty under §56-7-105 on bad-faith refusal — substantial but discretionary and strictly construed.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Discretionary fees under §56-7-105 (court or jury discretion).

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: Formal demand + 60-day wait required under §56-7-105 for statutory penalty (though contract claim itself not gated).

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: TDCI Consumer Insurance Services mediates and enforces fines/C&D; no individual adjudication mechanism.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Recent appellate activity (Riad, Lindenberg) opened door to common-law remedies alongside statutory — modestly broadening.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Independent first-party tort (Arnold) PLUS Ch. 541 statutory PRoA PLUS Ch. 542 prompt-pay; multi-tool regime.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Arnold v. Nat'l Cnty. Mut. (Tex. 1987); Tex. Ins. Code §541.151

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Stowers doctrine — landmark national standard; insurer liable for full excess judgment when ordinarily prudent insurer would accept within-limits demand.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Stowers v. Am. Indem. (Tex. 1929); In re Farmers Tex. (Tex. 2021)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: Ch. 541 broad PRoA with treble for knowing violations + Ch. 542 prompt-pay PRoA + DTPA crossover; reaches first- and third-party. Modestly hurt by §542A 60-day notice for residential property.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: No reasonable basis + knew or should have known (objective-leaning), but bona fide coverage dispute defeats and no failure-to-investigate claim per Castañeda.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Universe Life v. Giles (Tex. 1997); Provident Am. v. Castañeda (Tex. 1998)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages including loss of use, lost profits, and excess judgment in third-party context; mental anguish requires willful/grossly negligent + physical injury.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: J&D Towing v. Am. Alternative (Tex. 2016); Arnold (Tex. 1987)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on tort claims with clear & convincing evidence of fraud, malice, or gross negligence; statutory caps under §41.008.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 18% statutory interest on overdue claims under §542.060 PLUS treble damages under §541.152 for knowing violations.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: One-way fees under §541.152, §542A.007, and §38.001(b)(8) for breach of contract — broadly available.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: 60-day pre-suit notice under §541.154 / §542A.003 for residential property claims; not gating all claims but adds friction.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: TDI robust — complaints feed enforcement, publishes complaint ratios, OPIC statutory consumer advocate; no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: HB 1774 / §542A (2017) substantially narrowed first-party residential property claims; Castañeda and progeny restrict scope; Rodriguez v. Safeco (2024) further refined prompt-pay.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Beck v. Farmers held first-party bad faith is contract claim only — no independent tort. Significant doctrinal limitation.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Beck v. Farmers Ins. Exch. (Utah 1985)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Tort cause of action recognized for third-party context due to insurer's fiduciary duty to defend.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Beck v. Farmers, 701 P.2d at 799
