f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No PRoA under §31A-26-303; §31A-26-301 timely-pay also no PRoA per Saleh.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Saleh v. Farmers Ins. (Utah 2006); Utah Code §31A-26-303

f3_liability_standard.rationale: First-party (contract): breach of implied covenant of good faith; third-party: fiduciary duty — moderate standards.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages may exceed policy limits per Black v. Allstate; emotional distress available subject to foreseeability.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Black v. Allstate (Utah 2004); Beck (Utah 1985)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitives only on independent tort, not breach of contract — first-party bad faith as contract claim therefore largely lacks punitive route.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory multiplier or percent penalty for bad faith.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Available on breach of implied covenant of good faith, but discretionary in contract context.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Utah ID Consumer Services moderate — fines + C&D, no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Beck remains controlling; no major recent narrowing or broadening.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Independent tort cause of action for first-party bad faith with reasonable-basis + knowing/reckless standard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Bushey v. Allstate (Vt. 1995); Town of Ira (Vt. 2014)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Recognized in Myers; insurer liable for excess judgment when intentionally disregards insured's financial interests.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Myers v. Ambassador Ins. (Vt. 1986)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No PRoA under §4724 (Hamill); Consumer Fraud Act applicability to insurance is open question.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: No reasonable basis + insurer knew or recklessly disregarded — Anderson-style.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages routinely awarded in tort; emotional distress recoverable but not always.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Phillips v. Aetna (D. Vt. 1979); Buote v. Verizon (D. Vt. 2003)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on showing of malice, gross misconduct, or reckless disregard plus outrageous conduct.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: Davis v. Liberty Mut. (D. Vt. 1998); Monahan (Vt. 2005)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No statutory multiplier or percent penalty. Stat interest 12% only.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Discretionary under 'dominating reasons of justice' standard; not routinely awarded.
f7_attorney_fees.cite: Concord Gen. Mut. v. Woods (Vt. 2003); Monahan (Vt. 2005)

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DFR Insurance Consumer Services moderate; fines but no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; Bushey and Murphy (2014) maintain fairly debatable doctrine without major recent shift.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: No independent first-party tort; bad faith is contract claim only under implied covenant of good faith.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Botkin v. Donegal Mut. (W.D. Va. 2011); A-View Est. Tr. (Va. Cir. 2021)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Recognized cause of action for excess judgment when insurer fails to settle within limits, but requires clear & convincing evidence of insurer's intentional disregard.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Aetna Cas. v. Price (Va. 1966); State Farm v. Floyd (Va. 1988)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: §38.2-209 provides limited fee remedy in declaratory judgment action; capped at $3,500 disputes for double-recovery; no broad PRoA.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: Va. Code §38.2-209; Sustainable Sea Prod. (E.D. Va. 2022)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Reasonableness standard considering coverage interpretation, investigation, and underlying merits — moderate-to-difficult.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages available including excess judgment; no separate tort but contract remedies allow general/consequential.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: TIG Ins. v. Alfa Laval (E.D. Va. 2008); Meccia (Va. Cir. 1987)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Punitive damages unavailable in bad-faith cases.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: A-View Est. Tr. (Va. Cir. 2021); Harris v. USAA (Va. Cir. 1994)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: §38.2-209 double recovery + fees but capped at $3,500 disputes — very narrow penalty teeth.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Fees available under §38.2-209 in declaratory judgment if insurer not in good faith — capped/limited statutory route.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: Bureau of Insurance Property & Casualty Consumer Services moderate; fines + C&D, no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; no major recent direction; courts continue to disfavor free-standing bad-faith tort claims.
f10_recent_appellate_trend.cite: Liberty Ins. Corp. v. Talley (E.D. Va. 2023)

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Robust common-law tort PLUS IFCA statutory PRoA PLUS CPA route — strongest combined first-party regime in nation.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Coventry v. Am. States (Wash. 1998); RCW 48.30.015; Hangman Ridge (Wash. 1986)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Common-law tort + coverage-by-estoppel remedy under Kirk; CPA also reaches third-party scenarios; broader than typical Stowers.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Kirk v. Mt. Airy Ins. (Wash. 1998); St. Paul v. Onvia (Wash. 2008)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: IFCA broad PRoA with treble and one-way fees, augmented by CPA route; Perez-Crisantos (2017) limited IFCA to coverage/benefits denials, costing top score.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Unreasonable, frivolous, or unfounded breach — objective test; insured may prevail under CPA regardless of correctness of coverage decision.
f3_liability_standard.cite: St. Paul v. Onvia (Wash. 2008); P.E.L. v. Premera (Wash. 2023)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential, emotional distress, plus coverage-by-estoppel remedy effectively expands liability beyond policy limits.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Coventry (Wash. 1998); Kirk (Wash. 1998); Schmidt v. Coogan (Wash. 2004)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: True punitives generally unavailable in WA, but IFCA treble damages function as exemplary substitute.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: Treble damages (3x) under IFCA — among the most powerful in the nation.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Mandatory one-way fees + statutory litigation costs under IFCA; CPA also provides fees.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: 20-day pre-suit notice to insurer AND OIC required under IFCA — modest cure period, narrower than FL/GA.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: OIC robust — investigates, fines, restitution, license action; statutorily integrated as IFCA pre-suit channel.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Perez-Crisantos (2017) significantly narrowed IFCA scope to coverage/benefits denials, excluding broader UCSPA violations.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party tort recognized; Hayseeds remedy strong (fees + net economic loss + ED); statutory UTPA route also available with general business practice requirement.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Loudin v. Nat'l Liab. (W. Va. 2011); Hayseeds v. State Farm (W. Va. 1986); Elmore (W. Va. 1998)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Post-2005 reform eliminated third-party PRoA under UTPA (§33-11-4a); third-party Jenkins-line bad faith largely gone for direct claims.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: W. Va. Code §33-11-4a; State ex rel. Nationwide v. Kaufman (W. Va. 2008)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: WVUTPA PRoA exists for first-party with general business practice requirement; third-party PRoA eliminated 2005.
f2_statutory_proa.cite: W. Va. Code §33-11-4a; Mills v. Watkins (W. Va. 2003)

f3_liability_standard.rationale: No reasonable basis + general business practice (multiple-violation) for UTPA claims; common-law more lenient.
f3_liability_standard.cite: Mills v. Watkins (W. Va. 2003); Hayseeds (W. Va. 1986)

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages broadly available under Hayseeds and net-economic-loss framework.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: Hayseeds v. State Farm (W. Va. 1986)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available under UTPA; for property settlement requires high actual-malice threshold per McCormick.
f5_punitive_damages.cite: McCormick v. Allstate (W. Va. 1998); Hayseeds (W. Va. 1986)

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No multiplier or fixed % penalty; Hayseeds attorney fees + net economic loss function as common-law penalty.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Hayseeds common-law fees broadly available when insured substantially prevails — robust route.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice for first-party; UTPA general-business-practice requirement is substantive not procedural; underlying claim must be resolved before UTPA suit.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: OIC Consumer Services moderate; fines + C&D, no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Post-2005 third-party reform was a major narrowing; subsequent decade has trended toward stabilization but reform legacy persists.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Anderson v. Continental — landmark common-law first-party bad-faith tort with classic 'no reasonable basis + knowledge or reckless disregard' standard.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Anderson v. Cont'l Ins. (Wis. 1978); Jones v. Secura (Wis. 2002)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Mowry/Roehl recognize third-party bad faith for failure to settle and even within deductible context — moderately broad.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Mowry v. Badger State Mut. (Wis. 1986); Roehl Transp. v. Liberty Mut. (Wis. 2010)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No PRoA under UCSPA §628.34; §628.46 prompt-pay 12% interest is regulatory, not a true PRoA.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Anderson canonical: no reasonable basis + knowledge or reckless disregard — defines middle anchor.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential damages broadly available; emotional distress recoverable when severe + substantial other damage.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: DeChant v. Monarch Life (Wis. 1996); Jones (Wis. 2002)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available with intentional breach + oppression/fraud/malice (Mid-Continent v. Straka standard); no statutory cap unique to bad faith.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: 12% prompt-pay interest under §628.46 only; no multiplier or % penalty.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: DeChant recognizes fees as part of damages proximately resulting from bad faith — broader than discretionary, narrower than mandatory.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: OCI Consumer Affairs moderate — fines, no individual adjudication.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Roehl (2010) extended bad faith to within-deductible context — modestly broadening; doctrine otherwise stable.

f1a_first_party_cause.rationale: Common-law first-party bad-faith tort recognized with objective reasonable-basis test.
f1a_first_party_cause.cite: Ahrenholtz v. Time Ins. (Wyo. 1998); Cathcart v. State Farm (Wyo. 2005)

f1b_third_party_cause.rationale: Feinman silent on Wyoming third-party doctrine specifics; default to standard Stowers-equivalent recognized in most states.
f1b_third_party_cause.cite: Wyoming common law (Feinman silent)

f2_statutory_proa.rationale: No PRoA under UCSPA §26-13-124. Statute regulatory only.

f3_liability_standard.rationale: Objective fairly-debatable standard with knowledge or reckless disregard requirement.

f4_extracontractual_damages.rationale: Consequential and emotional distress damages available, ED conditioned on additional economic loss.
f4_extracontractual_damages.cite: State Farm v. Shrader (Wyo. 1994); Farmers Ins. v. Shirley (Wyo. 1998)

f5_punitive_damages.rationale: Available on evil intent, wanton disregard, or gross/outrageous conduct — moderate cap-free standard.

f6_statutory_penalty.rationale: No multiplier or % penalty. Statutory interest 10% only.

f7_attorney_fees.rationale: Available under §26-15-124(c) when insurer unreasonably or without cause refuses to pay full covered loss — one-way but not robust.

f8_pre_suit_barriers.rationale: No pre-suit notice or cure requirement.

f9_admin_remedy.rationale: DOI weak/mediation-focused; fines but no adjudication mechanism.

f10_recent_appellate_trend.rationale: Stable; doctrine continues under Ahrenholtz/Cathcart line without major recent direction.
